rt2-8-1.title: Hands-on: land every section in the density window
rt2-8-1.intro: Drive exposure E = A x t until film density lands inside the 2.0-3.0 readable slice - the same characteristic-curve budget that forces multi-film loads on stepped parts. Then rebuild the exposure after 30 days of Ir-192 decay, the very shift along log-E a slower film demands.

tasks.label: Set exposure time to land density between 2.0 and 3.0
tasks.hint: Density rises with exposure E = A x t - nudge the time slider until D sits inside the green window.

tasks.label: Press '30 days later', then re-enter the density window
tasks.hint: Ir-192 loses about a quarter of its activity in 30 days - stretch the time roughly a third longer to rebuild the same exposure.

rt2-8-2.title: Hands-on: beat the Ug cap and place the IQI
rt2-8-2.intro: Your shot opens with a 3.8 mm source, a short SOD, and a 50 mm film gap - work the geometry chain (film in contact first, then stretch the distance) until Ug = f x OFD / SOD meets the 0.5 mm code cap. Then grade the setup the way the code does: wire IQI on the source side.

tasks.label: Drive Ug at or under the 0.5 mm code cap
tasks.hint: Ug = f x OFD / SOD - close the object-to-film gap first, then pull the source back until the readout passes.

tasks.label: Place the wire IQI on the source-side weld surface
tasks.hint: Drag the IQI onto the weld's source-side surface - film-side placement is rejected, just as the code intends.

rt2-8-3.title: Hands-on: triangulate flaw depth with a 45-degree beam
rt2-8-3.intro: A single radiograph hides depth; the tube-shift method recovers it from similar triangles. Work the same triangle with the follow-up tool RT interpreters call for - a 45-degree angle beam whose sound path pins each hidden flaw's depth and position.

tasks.label: Peak the first echo and mark the flaw
tasks.hint: Slide slowly - the echo peaks when the beam centreline crosses the hole; press Mark within 4 mm of the peak.

tasks.label: Find and mark the second hidden flaw
tasks.hint: At 45 degrees the flaw lies as far ahead of the wedge as it is deep - read the drawn beam path's triangle.
